comments.body: Thanks. But what's the use of the in, out and err methods that are available to us publicly ? They all return instances of FileDescriptor through which can again do pretty much little ?

answers.body:
As the JavaDoc says:

Instances of the file descriptor class serve as an opaque handle to the underlying machine-specific structure representing an open file, an open socket, or another source or sink of bytes. The main practical use for a file descriptor is to create a FileInputStream or FileOutputStream to contain it.

Applications should not create their own file descriptors.

They are therefore a behind-the-scenes implementation detail for the JDK to plug in to the underlying OS

We can obtain it in other ways as well, such as referring to static constants out, err and in in FileDescriptor class. Also, you might want to provide your own System.out implementation for example performance reasons. By default it does for example handling of unicode which you might not need if you strive for performance. In that case you can still access the standard stdout using FileDescriptor.out.

title: What's the use of FileDescriptor class in Java and its in,out and err methods?
body:
What's the use of the FileDescriptor class in Java ? We can only obtain it with the getFD() methods on streams and then we can use the obtained FD to create another instance of, say, FileInputStream which we can also do by creating a FileInputStream object with the same file name (instead of using its FD).

Also, the methods in, out and err. what use can they be put to ?
